places.name: El Rinconcillo
places.price: $$ — tapas tallied in chalk on the bar
places.reviewSummary: Founded in 1670, this is the oldest tavern in Seville, all dark wood, hanging hams and tiled walls. Come for classic spinach with chickpeas and the theatre of it.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the atmosphere and history; some note it gets crowded and touristy at peak hours.
places.localTip: Stand at the bar rather than taking a table upstairs — it's cheaper, livelier, and waiters chalk your tab right on the wood.
places.story: This bar has poured wine since 1670, which makes it older than most countries' constitutions. The waiters still scrawl your bill in chalk on the worn wooden counter, just as they did three and a half centuries ago. Order the espinacas con garbanzos — spinach and chickpeas — a recipe that arrived in Seville with the Moors.
places.highlight: Don't miss the espinacas con garbanzos.

places.name: Eslava
places.reviewSummary: A neighbourhood favourite that turned tapas into something inventive without losing its soul. The slow-cooked egg on mushroom cake is legendary.
places.googleReviewSummary: Consistently praised for creative, affordable dishes; the main complaint is the long wait with no reservations for the bar.
places.localTip: Arrive right when they open at 12:30 or 8pm — there's no booking for the bar and the queue forms fast.
places.story: Eslava sits on a quiet square in the San Lorenzo barrio, and it's where Seville's tapas got their modern reinvention. Their slow-cooked egg over mushroom and a sticky honey-glazed pork rib have won national tapas competitions outright. Locals will tell you it's the proof that the best food in Seville isn't always near the cathedral.
places.highlight: Don't miss the cigarro para Bécquer, a crispy honey-glazed cigar of pork.

places.name: Las Golondrinas
places.reviewSummary: A two-floor Triana classic loved for its grilled mushrooms, punta de solomillo and unfussy charm. Pure local energy.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners rave about the champiñones and friendly buzz; some say it's tight on space and noisy.
places.localTip: Order the champiñones a la plancha and the punta de solomillo al whisky — both house signatures locals queue for.
places.story: In the heart of Triana, the old ceramic and sailors' quarter, Las Golondrinas has fed generations of Sevillanos. The name means 'the swallows', the birds that return to Seville each spring. It's the kind of place where you elbow in at the bar and leave smelling faintly of grilled garlic and happiness.

places.name: La Brunilda
places.reviewSummary: A bright, buzzy little bar serving some of the most inventive tapas in the old town, from risotto to slow-cooked pork cheek.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beloved for bold flavours and value; reviewers warn it's tiny and fills instantly, so expect a wait.
places.localTip: Go for an early lunch or get there before 8:30pm for dinner — they don't take bookings and the line is real.
places.story: Tucked down a narrow Arenal lane near the bullring, La Brunilda became a cult favourite almost overnight. Despite the fame it stays small, just a handful of tables and a marble bar. The pork cheek in red wine practically falls apart when you look at it.
places.highlight: Don't miss the carrillada — pork cheek braised in red wine.

places.name: Freiduría Puerta de la Carne
places.address: Calle Santa María la Blanca, 34, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A classic pescaíto frito shop where Andalusian fried fish comes wrapped in paper, crisp and light. A Seville ritual.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for fresh, greaseless fried fish; some note the seating is limited and it's mainly takeaway.
places.localTip: Order a surtido (mixed cone) of cazón, boquerones and calamari to take to a bench in the nearby gardens.
places.story: Fried fish is religion in Seville, and this freiduría near the old Puerta de la Carne gate has kept the faith for decades. The secret is chickpea-flour dusting and oil hot enough to seal the fish in seconds, so it stays crisp and never oily. Sevillanos eat it standing, with a squeeze of lemon and a cold beer.

places.name: Bar Las Teresas
places.price: $$ — jamón and sherry in a tiled bar
places.reviewSummary: A jewel-box of a bar in the old Jewish quarter, hams hanging from the ceiling and azulejo tiles everywhere. Famous for its jamón ibérico.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the postcard setting and ham; some find it pricey and touristy given the location.
places.localTip: Squeeze in at the bar and pair the jamón with a glass of manzanilla sherry — that's the local move.
places.story: Las Teresas has stood in the labyrinth of Santa Cruz since 1870, when this was the old Jewish quarter of Seville. The ceiling bristles with cured hams, and the walls are a riot of painted tiles. It's almost impossibly photogenic, but the jamón ibérico de bellota is the real reason to come.

places.name: Mercado Lonja del Barranco
places.reviewSummary: A restored 19th-century iron market on the Guadalquivir, now a buzzing food hall with stalls for everything from oysters to Iberian ham.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for variety and the riverside setting; some say prices are high and it gets touristy.
places.localTip: Grab a stool on the terrace facing the river at sunset — the Triana bridge view is worth the slightly inflated prices.
places.story: This airy iron-and-glass hall was designed in the 1880s, in the same era as Eiffel's great structures, as a fish market on the riverbank. Today it's a gourmet bazaar where you can graze your way through Andalusia in one meal. The terrace looks straight across the Guadalquivir to Triana.

places.name: La Azotea
places.address: Calle Jesús del Gran Poder, 31, 41002 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A contemporary tapas spot focused on seasonal market produce and fresh, well-plated dishes. A favourite of food-minded locals.
places.googleReviewSummary: Consistently praised for quality and presentation; reviewers note small premises and waits at busy times.
places.localTip: The tuna tataki and the seasonal fish specials change daily — ask the waiter what came in from the market.
places.story: La Azotea helped lead Seville's new wave of tapas, treating tradition with respect but never reverence. Everything starts at the market that morning, so the menu shifts with the seasons. It's the kind of place a Sevillano takes a friend to prove the city's food scene is more than fried fish and ham.

places.name: Confitería La Campana
places.reviewSummary: Seville's most famous pastry shop, open since 1885, on the corner where Calle Sierpes meets La Campana. Famous for its yemas and tocino de cielo.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for tradition and quality sweets; some say service at the counter can be brisk and busy.
places.localTip: Order a tocino de cielo (a silky caramel custard) and an espresso standing at the bar like the locals.
places.story: La Campana has sweetened Seville since 1885, and its corner is so iconic that the whole square took its name. The display cases gleam with yemas — candied egg-yolk sweets born in the city's convents. Generations of Sevillanos have started feast days and ended Sunday strolls right here.
places.highlight: Don't miss the tocino de cielo.

places.name: Virgen Coffee
places.reviewSummary: A small specialty coffee bar near Las Setas pulling some of the best espresso in the old town, with rotating single-origin beans.
places.googleReviewSummary: Coffee nerds praise the quality and friendly baristas; the main caveat is it's tiny with little seating.
places.localTip: Try a flat white and ask what single origin is on the bar that week — they roast and rotate often.
places.story: Seville runs on strong, dark café con leche, but Virgen brought third-wave specialty coffee to the old town. It's a tiny, bright spot just steps from the mushroom-shaped Setas. Come for a properly pulled espresso made by people who genuinely care about the bean.

places.name: Filo
places.reviewSummary: A relaxed, design-led café near the Alameda known for brunch plates, homemade cakes and good coffee. A laptop-and-latte kind of spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for cosy vibe and brunch; some note slower service when it's full.
places.localTip: Weekend brunch fills up — go midweek for a quiet corner and a slice of their carrot cake.
places.story: The Alameda de Hércules has long been Seville's bohemian heart, and Filo fits right in. It's a calm, light-filled spot where the city's creatives nurse coffees and pick at brunch. The cakes are all made in-house, and the carrot cake has a quiet following.

places.name: Bar Bistec
places.price: $ — coffee, beer and tapas in Triana
places.reviewSummary: A classic, tiled Triana corner bar good for morning coffee or an evening caña, with a leafy plaza terrace. Full of neighbourhood regulars.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for authentic Triana atmosphere and friendly staff; some note it can be busy and basic.
places.localTip: Take a table on the little plaza out front in the morning sun for coffee and toast with tomato.
places.story: Deep in Triana, away from the tourist crush, Bar Bistec is the sort of azulejo-tiled corner bar that anchors a neighbourhood. By day it's coffee and tostadas; by evening it's cold beer and montaditos. The plaza terrace catches the morning light beautifully.

places.name: Convento de San Leandro
places.address: Plaza de San Ildefonso, 1, 41003 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A working convent where cloistered nuns sell their famous yemas de San Leandro through a wooden turntable. A vanishing tradition.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find the lazy-Susan transaction charming and the sweets excellent; note limited hours and cash only.
places.localTip: Ring the bell, ask for a caja de yemas through the turntable, leave the coins, and the box rotates back to you — you never see the nun.
places.story: For centuries, Seville's cloistered nuns have funded their convents by making sweets, and at San Leandro you can still buy their famous yemas. You place your order and money on a wooden lazy-Susan called a torno, it spins into the cloister, and your box of candied egg-yolk sweets spins back out. You never see a face — only hear a soft voice say gracias.
places.highlight: Don't miss the yemas de San Leandro.

places.name: Heladería La Fiorentina
places.reviewSummary: A long-running ice cream parlour near the centre making rich, artisan gelato — a lifesaver in Seville's brutal summer heat.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the creamy textures and flavours; some say there's often a queue in summer evenings.
places.localTip: Try the turrón or pistachio, and go for the evening paseo when the whole city is out walking off the heat.
places.story: When Seville's summer pushes past 40 degrees, the entire city survives on ice cream, and La Fiorentina has been a refuge for decades. The gelato is dense and properly made in the Italian style. There's no better way to join the evening paseo than with a cone in hand.

places.name: Seville Cathedral
places.address: Av. de la Constitución, s/n, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The largest Gothic cathedral in the world, holding the tomb of Christopher Columbus. Built on the site of a great mosque.
places.googleReviewSummary: Universally awed by the scale and the Giralda climb; the main caveat is long queues — book ahead.
places.localTip: Buy a timed ticket online to skip the line, and climb the Giralda via its 35 gentle ramps rather than stairs.
places.story: When the people of Seville set out to build this cathedral in 1401, the legend goes they vowed to make it 'so beautiful that those who see it will think we are mad.' It is the largest Gothic church on Earth, and somewhere inside rest the bones of Christopher Columbus, carried by four heralds in bronze. The tower beside it, the Giralda, was once the minaret of the mosque that stood here.
places.highlight: Don't miss climbing the Giralda for the city view.

places.name: Real Alcázar of Seville
places.address: Patio de Banderas, s/n, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A breathtaking royal palace blending Moorish, Mudéjar and Renaissance styles, still used by the Spanish royal family. The oldest palace in Europe still in use.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are spellbound by the tilework and gardens; the only complaint is the crowds, so book early slots.
places.localTip: Reserve the first morning entry slot to have the Patio de las Doncellas almost to yourself, and don't rush the gardens.
places.story: The Alcázar has been a seat of power for over a thousand years, and it's the oldest royal palace still in use anywhere in Europe — Spain's king still stays here. Its carved plasterwork and gleaming tiles were made by Muslim craftsmen for a Christian king, Pedro the Cruel. Fans of Dorne in Game of Thrones will recognise the gardens instantly.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Patio de las Doncellas.

places.name: Plaza de España
places.address: Av. de Isabel la Católica, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A vast, semicircular plaza of brick, tile and canals built for the 1929 Ibero-American Exposition. One of Spain's most beautiful squares.
places.googleReviewSummary: Almost universally adored for its grandeur and tiled alcoves; busiest midday, so go early or at golden hour.
places.localTip: Hire a little rowboat on the canal, then find the tiled alcove for your home province — there's one for all 48 provinces of Spain.
places.story: This sweeping half-moon of brick and ceramic was built in 1928 for a world's fair, meant to symbolise Spain embracing its former colonies, its arms open toward the river and the Americas. Around its base sit 48 tiled alcoves, one for every Spanish province. Star Wars filmed scenes of the planet Naboo right here.
places.highlight: Don't miss finding the tiled alcoves at golden hour.

places.name: Setas de Sevilla (Metropol Parasol)
places.address: Plaza de la Encarnación, s/n, 41003 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The world's largest wooden structure, a wavy mushroom-shaped canopy with a rooftop walkway over the old town. Locals call it Las Setas.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the rooftop views and sunset light show; some find the ticket area confusing and the design divisive.
places.localTip: Go for the sunset slot — your ticket includes a drink, and the walkway puts the cathedral and rooftops in perfect light.
places.story: When workers dug here to build a car park, they hit a Roman city, so the plan changed entirely. The result is Las Setas — 'the mushrooms' — the largest wooden structure in the world, undulating above a preserved Roman ruin in the basement. Sevillanos argued about it for years, but the rooftop walkway at sunset wins everyone over.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sunset light show on the walkway.

places.name: Torre del Oro
places.address: Paseo de Cristóbal Colón, s/n, 41001 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A 13th-century Moorish watchtower on the Guadalquivir, once part of the city's defences and the gateway for New World treasure.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved as a riverside landmark and photo spot; some say the inside naval museum is small.
places.localTip: You don't have to go inside — the best view is from the Triana bridge with the tower mirrored in the river at dusk.
places.story: The Tower of Gold has guarded the Guadalquivir since around 1220, when the Almohads built it to control the river. Its name may come from the golden tiles that once clad it, or from the New World silver and gold that flowed past it into Seville's treasury. A great chain once stretched from here across the river to block enemy ships.

places.name: Plaza de Toros de la Real Maestranza
places.address: Paseo de Cristóbal Colón, 12, 41001 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: One of the oldest and most beautiful bullrings in Spain, a perfect white-and-ochre oval begun in 1761. The spiritual home of bullfighting.
places.googleReviewSummary: Tours praised as informative and beautiful even for those uneasy about bullfighting; some find it short for the price.
places.localTip: Take the guided tour for the museum and chapel where matadors pray before a fight, even if you'd never attend a corrida.
places.story: La Maestranza is bullfighting's cathedral, a luminous oval of white arches and golden sand begun in 1761. For Sevillanos, the April feria and the bullfighting season are woven into the city's identity, for better and worse. Tucked behind the ring is the tiny chapel where matadors pray before walking out onto the sand.

places.name: Casa de Pilatos
places.reviewSummary: A sumptuous 16th-century Andalusian palace mixing Mudéjar tilework with Italian Renaissance, still owned by an aristocratic family.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it a quieter, equally stunning alternative to the Alcázar; the only caveat is fewer English signs.
places.localTip: It's far less crowded than the Alcázar — come midday when the big sites are jammed and you'll have the tiled courtyards nearly to yourself.
places.story: Legend says a 16th-century duke returned from the Holy Land and modelled this palace on Pontius Pilate's house in Jerusalem, which gave it the name. Its central courtyard is a dazzling collision of Sevillian tiles, Roman statues and Gothic arches. It's one of the city's most beautiful interiors, yet most visitors walk right past it.
places.highlight: Don't miss the tiled central courtyard.

places.name: Barrio de Santa Cruz
places.address: Barrio de Santa Cruz, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The old Jewish quarter, a maze of whitewashed lanes, flower-filled patios and tiny squares behind the cathedral. Seville at its most romantic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beloved for atmosphere and charm; the only gripe is it's easy to get lost and crowded near the cathedral.
places.localTip: Get deliberately lost in the early morning before tour groups arrive — find Plaza de Doña Elvira and Calle Agua's hidden patios.
places.story: This labyrinth of narrow white lanes was Seville's Jewish quarter until the late Middle Ages. The streets were kept tight and twisting to hold off the summer heat, and many still feel barely wider than your shoulders. Wander long enough and you'll stumble on orange-scented squares that feel unchanged in five centuries.

places.name: Mercado de Triana
places.reviewSummary: A lively neighbourhood market built over the ruins of a castle, with produce stalls, tapas bars and a ceramics history exhibit.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for authentic atmosphere and fresh food; some say a few stalls lean touristy now.
places.localTip: Eat at one of the small tapas counters inside for market-fresh seafood, then visit the Castillo de San Jorge ruins beneath it for free.
places.story: Triana's market sits on the foundations of the Castillo de San Jorge, once the grim headquarters of the Spanish Inquisition. Today it's all glistening fish, hams and chatter, with little bars where you eat what was on ice that morning. Downstairs, you can still walk through the castle's excavated ruins.

places.name: Calle Feria
places.reviewSummary: Seville's oldest street, lined with veteran bars and home to El Jueves flea market every Thursday. Authentic working-class Seville.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals praise the unpretentious bars and market; visitors note it's gritty and not polished — that's the point.
places.localTip: Come Thursday morning for El Jueves, the city's centuries-old flea market, then duck into Bar La Cantina for vermouth.
places.story: Calle Feria is said to be the oldest street in Seville, and every Thursday it hosts El Jueves, a flea market that's run for over 700 years. This is the Feria barrio, proud, scruffy and utterly unfiltered. The bars here pour vermouth and serve tapas to people who've drunk in them their whole lives.

places.name: Calle Betis
places.reviewSummary: A row of colourful houses and terraces along the Triana riverbank, with the best postcard view of Seville across the water.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the views and buzzy bars; some warn it gets rowdy and packed on weekend nights.
places.localTip: Sit on the river wall with a beer at sunset — the cathedral and Torre del Oro light up across the Guadalquivir.
places.story: Calle Betis takes its name from the Roman name for the river, the Baetis. The painted houses face directly across the Guadalquivir to the old city, giving Triana the best free view in Seville. By night the terraces fill, and the whole bank hums with guitar and laughter.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunset from the river wall.

places.name: Cerámica Santa Ana
places.reviewSummary: A historic Triana ceramics shop and workshop selling the hand-painted azulejo tiles the neighbourhood has made for centuries.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for authentic, beautiful tiles and helpful staff; some note prices reflect the handcraft.
places.localTip: Buy a single hand-painted tile as a featherweight, unbreakable-in-a-suitcase souvenir of Triana's pottery tradition.
places.story: Triana has been Seville's pottery quarter for a thousand years, its clay dug straight from the riverbank. Cerámica Santa Ana keeps the tradition alive, its facade a glorious patchwork of painted azulejos. The tiles you see across Seville's plazas and palaces were born in workshops just like this one.

places.name: Plaza de la Alfalfa
places.address: Plaza de la Alfalfa, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A lively old-town square ringed with tapas bars where Sevillanos gather, especially for a Sunday pre-lunch vermouth.
places.googleReviewSummary: Loved for its local, un-touristy buzz; some say it's busiest and noisiest at weekends.
places.localTip: Come Sunday around 1pm for the vermut tradition — locals spill out of the bars onto the square with little glasses of vermouth.
places.story: Alfalfa was once a Roman forum and later a hay market, which gave the square its name. Today it's one of the old town's most local hangouts, ringed by bars that haven't changed in decades. On Sundays the whole square turns into an open-air vermouth party before lunch.

places.name: Parque de María Luisa
places.address: Paseo de las Delicias, s/n, 41013 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Seville's lush green lung, full of tiled fountains, ponds, palms and white doves. The city's favourite place to escape the heat.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beloved for shade, beauty and tranquillity; the only gripe is some quiet corners need upkeep.
places.localTip: Rent a four-person pedal-car or just find the Glorieta de Bécquer monument under the trees for shade and doves.
places.story: This park was the private garden of a royal palace until a princess gave it to the city in 1893. For the 1929 fair it was filled with tiled fountains, pavilions and hidden glorietas dedicated to Sevillian poets. On a fierce summer day, its shade and birdsong feel like a small miracle.

places.name: Kayak Sevilla (Guadalquivir)
places.address: Calle Betis, s/n (river access), 41010 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Guided kayak trips along the Guadalquivir, seeing the Torre del Oro and Triana from the water. A refreshing, different angle on the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the unique viewpoint and friendly guides; some note booking ahead is essential in summer.
places.localTip: Book a late-afternoon paddle to catch the riverside monuments glowing in the low golden sun.
places.story: The Guadalquivir is the only great navigable river in Spain, and it once carried galleons home from the Americas laden with silver. Paddling its calm waters today, you glide past the Torre del Oro and under the Triana bridge with the whole city rising on the bank. It's the only way to see Seville the way the sailors first did.

places.name: EME Catedral Rooftop
places.reviewSummary: A chic hotel rooftop bar staring straight at the Giralda, perfect for a sunset cocktail almost at eye level with the cathedral.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the cathedral views; the common caveat is steep drink prices and a dress-up vibe.
places.localTip: Go just before sunset for a drink, when the Giralda turns gold — the view, not the cocktail, is what you're paying for.
places.story: There's no closer place to toast the Giralda than this rooftop, where you stand almost level with the great bell tower. As the sun drops, the cathedral's stone glows amber and the bells ring out across the rooftops. It's pricey, but the view is one of Seville's great showstoppers.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Giralda at sunset.

places.name: Devour Seville Tapas Tour
places.address: Meeting point near Plaza Nueva, 41001 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A small-group food tour through historic bars, tasting tapas, sherry and Iberian ham with a local guide. A delicious crash course in the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the guides, food and history; the only caveat is it's a splurge versus eating solo.
places.localTip: Book the evening tour and skip dinner — you'll be thoroughly fed across four or five stops.
places.story: Seville invented the tapa, supposedly when a king was served a glass of sherry with a slice of ham laid on top as a lid — tapa means 'cover'. A guided crawl unlocks the unspoken etiquette of the city's bars: what to order, where to stand, how to pay. You leave understanding Seville's whole rhythm of eating.

places.name: Seville Cathedral Rooftops Tour
places.address: Av. de la Constitución, s/n, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A guided climb across the cathedral's vaults and rooftops, normally closed to the public, with views over the buttresses and city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find it a magical behind-the-scenes experience; note it books out and isn't for those wary of heights.
places.localTip: Reserve weeks ahead — these small-group rooftop tours sell out fast and run in several languages.
places.story: Most visitors only see the cathedral from below, but this tour takes you out onto its roof, walking above the largest Gothic church on Earth. You stand among flying buttresses and pinnacles, tracing how medieval masons engineered the impossible. Up here, the Giralda looms close and the whole city spreads beneath you.

places.name: Horse Carriage at Plaza de España
places.address: Av. de Isabel la Católica, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A classic horse-and-carriage ride through María Luisa park and past Plaza de España, an old-fashioned, romantic way to see the monuments.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find it charming and relaxing; some flag the price and to agree the fare and route before setting off.
places.localTip: Agree the price up front — there's an official tariff — and ask to loop through the shady park, not just the busy avenues.
places.story: Horse carriages have clopped through Seville since the 19th century, and they remain part of the city's romance, especially during the April feria. A ride loops you through the palm-shaded María Luisa park and along the grand sweep of Plaza de España. The unhurried pace lets the city's faded grandeur sink in.

places.name: Vinería San Telmo Tasting
places.address: Paseo de Catalina de Ribera, 4, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A warm wine bar near the Murillo gardens pairing Andalusian wines and sherries with creative tapas. A relaxed introduction to local wine.
places.googleReviewSummary: Praised for wine knowledge and inventive tapas; some note it's snug and popular, so reserve.
places.localTip: Ask the staff to walk you from a dry fino to a sweet Pedro Ximénez sherry — they love guiding the journey.
places.story: Sherry is Andalusia's great gift to wine, aged under a living veil of yeast in nearby Jerez. At San Telmo you can taste your way from a bone-dry fino to a syrupy Pedro Ximénez, each paired with a clever small plate. It's the easiest, most delicious way to understand why locals drink sherry, not just sangria.

places.name: Museo de Bellas Artes de Sevilla
places.price: $ — small entry, free for EU citizens
places.reviewSummary: One of Spain's finest art museums, set in a former convent and rich in Sevillian Golden Age painting — Murillo, Zurbarán and Valdés Leal.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it an underrated gem with a beautiful building; some wish for more English labels.
places.localTip: EU passport holders get in free; don't skip the tiled main staircase and the church-hall room of Murillos.
places.story: Housed in a serene 17th-century convent, this is considered second only to the Prado among Spain's art museums. Its walls hold the soft, glowing saints of Murillo, Seville's own Golden Age master, born here in 1617. The old convent church, now a gallery, is itself a work of art.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Murillo room in the former church.

places.name: Museo del Baile Flamenco
places.address: Calle Manuel Rojas Marcos, 3, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A museum dedicated to the art of flamenco dance, founded by legendary dancer Cristina Hoyos, with nightly live shows in its courtyard.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the intimate shows and interactive exhibits; some find the museum part small for the ticket.
places.localTip: Combine the museum visit with an evening show in the atmospheric brick-vaulted patio downstairs.
places.story: Flamenco was born from the mingling of Roma, Moorish, Jewish and Andalusian cultures, and Seville is one of its cradles. This museum, created by the great dancer Cristina Hoyos, traces the art's gestures and history. Its underground patio hosts some of the most intimate live shows in the city.

places.name: Centro Andaluz de Arte Contemporáneo (CAAC)
places.reviewSummary: Andalusia's contemporary art museum, set inside a vast former Carthusian monastery where Columbus once stayed and was first buried.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the dramatic monastery setting; some say the art selection varies and signage is sparse.
places.localTip: Go on the free-entry hours and wander the monastery gardens and old ceramic kilns even if modern art isn't your thing.
places.story: This monastery on the Cartuja island sheltered Christopher Columbus while he planned his voyages, and his body even rested here for a time before crossing the Atlantic. Later it became a ceramics factory, and you can still see the great bottle kilns. Today contemporary art fills its ancient cloisters in a striking collision of old and new.

places.name: Palacio de las Dueñas
places.reviewSummary: The exquisite Seville home of the late Duchess of Alba, full of art, antiques and a famously beautiful courtyard and gardens.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the intimate, lived-in feel and patios; some note the audio guide adds a lot to the visit.
places.localTip: The audio guide is excellent — it tells the colourful story of the Duchess of Alba, one of Spain's most storied aristocrats.
places.story: This was the cherished Seville home of the Duchess of Alba, who held more noble titles than anyone alive and was a beloved national character. The poet Antonio Machado was born in a corner of this very palace. Its courtyards drip with bougainvillea and centuries of accumulated treasure.

places.name: Palacio de la Condesa de Lebrija
places.reviewSummary: A 16th-century palace whose floors are paved with Roman mosaics rescued from the ruins of nearby Itálica. A treasure-house of antiquity.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are amazed by the mosaics; some say the upstairs guided tour is worth the extra cost.
places.localTip: Pay the small extra for the upper-floor guided tour to see the countess's private library and collections.
places.story: An eccentric, archaeology-mad countess transformed this palace at the turn of the 20th century, paving its courtyards with genuine Roman mosaics she dug up at nearby Itálica. The main patio floor is a vast, intact mosaic that once carpeted a Roman villa. Few house museums anywhere are paved in two-thousand-year-old art.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Roman mosaic courtyard.

places.name: Iglesia del Salvador
places.address: Plaza del Salvador, s/n, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Seville's second-largest church, a riot of golden Baroque altarpieces built atop the city's main former mosque.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers are stunned by the gilded retablos; some say the square outside is as much a draw as the church.
places.localTip: Your cathedral ticket usually includes entry here — and the steps outside are a classic spot for an evening beer.
places.story: El Salvador rose on the foundations of Seville's main mosque, and in its courtyard you can still see the old Islamic ablutions fountain and a patch of orange trees. Inside, the Baroque altarpieces blaze with gold so dense it almost overwhelms. Outside, the church steps are one of the city's favourite spots for a sunset caña.

places.name: Hospital de los Venerables
places.address: Plaza de los Venerables, 8, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A beautifully restored 17th-century home for elderly priests, now an art centre with a sublime Baroque chapel and Velázquez works.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the peaceful sunken patio and frescoes; some say it's small but exquisite.
places.localTip: Look for the trick fountain in the sunken patio, designed so water appears to flow uphill toward the entrance.
places.story: Hidden in the heart of Santa Cruz, this was once a hospice for aged and infirm priests, built in the 1670s. Its chapel is a jewel of Sevillian Baroque, every surface painted and gilded. The central patio sinks downward and hides a clever fountain engineered to make water seem to defy gravity.

places.name: Casa de la Memoria
places.reviewSummary: An intimate, purist flamenco venue in a 15th-century palace courtyard, prized for the quality of its dancers and singers over spectacle.
places.googleReviewSummary: Widely praised as the most authentic flamenco in the city; the caveat is small seating, so book days ahead.
places.localTip: Book online well in advance and arrive early for a front-row seat in the courtyard — there's no bad seat but the front is electric.
places.story: Set in the old stables of a 15th-century palace, Casa de la Memoria is where Sevillanos send friends who want the real thing. There's no dinner, no gimmicks — just a guitarist, a singer and a dancer a few feet away. When the dancer's heels hammer the wooden floor in the hush, you feel it in your chest.
places.highlight: Don't miss the closing soleá or bulería.

places.name: Tablao Flamenco Los Gallos
places.address: Plaza de Santa Cruz, 11, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: One of Seville's oldest tablaos, running since 1966, with a tightly packed room and high-calibre, fiery performances.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the powerful performers and historic vibe; some say the room is cramped and the drink is the only extra.
places.localTip: Sit toward the front edge of the small stage room — the dancers' footwork is best appreciated up close.
places.story: Los Gallos has staged flamenco on this little square in Santa Cruz since 1966, making it one of the city's oldest tablaos. Many of Spain's flamenco greats have stamped its boards over the decades. The room is small and intense, so the energy of singer and dancer hits you full force.

places.name: La Carbonería
places.reviewSummary: A rambling old coal yard turned bohemian bar famous for free, spontaneous flamenco most nights. Rough around the edges and beloved.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the free shows and atmosphere; some warn the performances are informal and drinks fund the night.
places.localTip: Arrive around 9–10pm, buy drinks generously (that's how it stays free), and don't expect a polished theatre show.
places.story: Once a coal merchant's yard — carbonería means coalhouse — this cavernous bar became a legendary haunt of flamenco and bohemian Seville. The flamenco is free, performed in a back room, and the magic is in its rawness. It feels like stumbling into a private juerga rather than a ticketed show.

places.name: Teatro de la Maestranza
places.address: Paseo de Cristóbal Colón, 22, 41001 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Seville's premier opera house and concert hall by the river, fitting for the city that inspired Carmen, Don Giovanni and The Barber of Seville.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the acoustics and programme; some note it's a dress-up evening and tickets sell out.
places.localTip: Catch a performance of an opera actually set in Seville — Carmen or The Barber of Seville hit differently here.
places.story: No city has inspired more operas than Seville — Carmen, Don Giovanni, The Barber of Seville and Fidelio are all set in its streets. This riverside opera house honours that legacy with world-class productions. To watch Carmen in the very city where the story unfolds is something special.

places.name: Naima Café Jazz
places.reviewSummary: A tiny, atmospheric jazz bar plastered with old photos and vinyl, hosting intimate live sets. A break from flamenco.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the cosy vibe and live music; some warn it's very small and fills fast on gig nights.
places.localTip: Check their schedule for live jazz nights and arrive early — there's barely room for a dozen people once the band sets up.
places.story: Named after the John Coltrane ballad, Naima is a pocket-sized shrine to jazz in the centre of Seville. Its walls are a collage of vintage photos and record sleeves. On live nights, the music spills out the door and a handful of lucky listeners pack in close to the players.

places.name: Calle Betis Bars
places.price: $ — beers and copas with a view
places.reviewSummary: The buzzing riverside bar strip in Triana where Seville drinks on warm nights, with the lit old city shimmering across the water.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the view and energy; some warn it's loud, packed and touristy on weekend nights.
places.localTip: Start at a terrace for the view, then move to the basement bars carved into the riverbank that open late.
places.story: When the sun goes down, Calle Betis becomes Seville's open-air living room, the whole strip glowing along the Guadalquivir. Locals and visitors mingle with beers in hand, the cathedral and Torre del Oro lit up across the river. Some bars burrow right into the old riverbank wall.

places.name: Alameda de Hércules
places.reviewSummary: Seville's most alternative nightlife hub, a long tree-lined plaza ringed with bars, terraces and clubs that fill late. The heart of going out.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the inclusive, lively, LGBTQ-friendly scene; some note it gets rowdy and noisy past midnight.
places.localTip: Bars don't get going until 11pm or later — start with a drink on a terrace and follow the crowd to the clubs.
places.story: The Alameda was laid out in 1574 as a grand public promenade, anchored by two Roman columns topped with statues of Hercules and Caesar. For decades it was the city's red-light fringe; today it's Seville's most bohemian, open-minded nightlife quarter. The terraces stay full until the small hours.

places.name: Sala Malandar
places.reviewSummary: A long-running indie club and live-music venue by the river, hosting concerts then DJ club nights for an alternative crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the gig line-ups and unpretentious vibe; some say it's dark and basic, as a good music club should be.
places.localTip: Check the gig calendar — many nights start as a concert and roll into a club night with the same ticket.
places.story: Malandar has been a fixture of Seville's indie and alternative scene for years, sitting along the old riverside Torneo wall. It books live bands early and turns into a sweaty club later. This is where you go to escape flamenco and feria and hear something with guitars and bass.

places.name: Buddha Bar Sevilla (La Terraza de EME)
places.reviewSummary: A stylish rooftop and lounge bar at the EME hotel facing the Giralda, for well-made cocktails and a dressed-up late crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the view and ambience; the recurring caveat is high prices and a smart dress code.
places.localTip: Cocktails are pricey but the Giralda view from the rooftop after dark is unbeatable — come for one perfect drink.
places.story: Right across from the cathedral, this rooftop lounge turns drinking into theatre, with the floodlit Giralda close enough to touch. The crowd dresses up, the cocktails are serious, and the city glitters below. It's where Seville goes to feel glamorous after dark.
places.highlight: Don't miss the floodlit Giralda from the terrace.

places.name: Antique Theatro
places.address: Calle Matemáticos Rey Pastor y Castro, s/n, 41092 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Seville's glossiest mega-club on Cartuja island, with a big outdoor terrace in summer and house and reggaeton until dawn.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the summer terrace and production; common gripes are pricey drinks and a strict, dressy door.
places.localTip: It barely fills before 2am and the outdoor terrace only runs in summer — dress smart, the door is selective.
places.story: On the Expo '92 island of Cartuja, Antique is where Seville's see-and-be-seen crowd dances until sunrise. In summer the action moves to a vast palm-fringed outdoor terrace. It's glossy, pricey and unapologetically a big night out.

places.name: Lo Nuestro
places.reviewSummary: A tiny, beloved Triana club where locals dance sevillanas and rumba to Andalusian music into the early hours. Pure local fun.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the authentic, joyful dancing; some say it's cramped, hot and not for those who want quiet.
places.localTip: Watch the regulars dance sevillanas, then let someone teach you the basic steps — they almost always will.
places.story: On Calle Betis, Lo Nuestro is where Sevillanos go to dance their own music, the sevillanas and rumbas of Andalusia. It's small, sweaty and joyous, full of people who've been coming for years. Next door, Bar El Rejoneo does the same for live Andalusian song.

places.name: Bar El Rejoneo
places.reviewSummary: A characterful Triana bar covered in bullfighting and flamenco memorabilia, with spontaneous live cante and a fervent local crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the genuine, passionate atmosphere; some say it's tight, smoky-feeling and not polished.
places.localTip: Come late, order a drink, and wait — the singing often erupts spontaneously rather than on a schedule.
places.story: El Rejoneo is plastered floor to ceiling with bullfighting posters and flamenco photos, a shrine to Andalusian passions. On good nights, the cante jondo — deep song — breaks out among the regulars without warning. It's raw, local and unforgettable when the magic strikes.

places.name: MercaderRon / Maquila Bar
places.reviewSummary: A modern craft-beer bar near Las Setas pouring local and Spanish microbrews, a refreshing change from the standard caña.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the rotating taps and knowledgeable staff; some note it's pricier than a normal beer bar.
places.localTip: Ask for a tasting flight of Andalusian craft beers — the staff are happy to guide you through the rotating taps.
places.story: Seville drinks oceans of cold, light Cruzcampo lager, so a proper craft-beer bar like Maquila feels almost rebellious. The taps rotate through Spanish and Andalusian microbreweries, and the staff know every pour. It's a low-key, modern counterpoint to the city's tile-and-sherry traditions.

places.name: Cerámica Triana Workshop
places.reviewSummary: Hands-on azulejo tile-painting workshops in Triana's historic pottery quarter, learning the techniques behind Seville's famous tiles.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the patient instruction and taking home their own tile; some say small groups book up fast.
places.localTip: Book ahead and wear clothes you don't mind getting glaze on — you'll paint a tile to take home as a keepsake.
places.story: Triana's clay has been shaped into tiles for over a thousand years, dug straight from the riverbank beneath your feet. In these workshops you learn the cuerda seca technique that gives Sevillian tiles their crisp coloured outlines. You leave with your own hand-painted azulejo, fired and glazed.

places.name: Taller Flamenco
places.reviewSummary: A long-running flamenco and Spanish-language school offering beginner-friendly dance classes for visitors. Learn to stamp and clap properly.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise warm teachers and a fun intro to the art; some note one-off classes are short.
places.localTip: Even a single drop-in class teaches you the compás (rhythm) and palmas (clapping) so flamenco shows make far more sense.
places.story: Flamenco looks like passion, but underneath it's fierce mathematics — intricate rhythmic cycles called compás. A class here lets you feel that structure in your own feet and hands, clapping the patterns and trying the footwork. You'll never watch a flamenco show the same way again.

places.name: Taller Andaluz de Cocina
places.address: Mercado de Triana, Calle San Jorge, 6, 41010 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: A cooking school inside Triana market that starts with a market tour, then teaches paella, gazpacho and tapas. Hands-on and delicious.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the market visit, friendly chefs and eating the results; the only caveat is the splurge price.
places.localTip: Choose the class that begins with the market walk — buying the ingredients first makes the whole thing click.
places.story: Right inside Triana's market, this school turns shopping into cooking, walking you past the stalls to choose the day's fish and produce. Then you make paella, salmorejo and tapas from scratch and sit down to eat what you cooked. It's the most delicious souvenir you can carry home — in your hands.
places.highlight: Don't miss making real Andalusian gazpacho from scratch.

places.name: Sherry Tasting at Casa de la Guitarra area
places.address: Calle Mesón del Moro, 12, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Guided sherry-tasting workshops in the old town that walk you through the styles of Jerez, from fino to oloroso to PX, with pairings.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the clear, fun education and generous pours; some say sessions are short but rich.
places.localTip: Go in with an empty palate — you'll taste five or six very different sherries and want to remember each one.
places.story: Sherry is one of the world's most misunderstood wines, ranging from bone-dry to lusciously sweet, all aged in the sun-baked bodegas of nearby Jerez. A guided tasting decodes the whole spectrum, from crisp fino to nutty oloroso to treacly Pedro Ximénez. By the end you'll understand why Andalusians are so devoted to it.

places.name: Plaza de España Bridges
places.address: Av. de Isabel la Católica, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The most photographed spot in Seville — the tiled bridges and curving facade of Plaza de España reflected in its canal.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call the photos spectacular; the only caveat is crowds, so come at opening or golden hour.
places.localTip: Arrive at 8–9am for soft light and almost no people, or return at golden hour for warm tones on the brick.
places.story: The four tiled bridges of Plaza de España represent the ancient kingdoms of Spain — Castile, León, Aragón and Navarre. Reflected in the canal beneath the curving brick facade, they create the city's signature photograph. Early morning, before the crowds, the whole plaza is yours.
places.highlight: Don't miss the reflection in the canal at golden hour.

places.name: Setas de Sevilla Mirador
places.address: Plaza de la Encarnación, s/n, 41003 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The undulating rooftop walkway of Las Setas gives the best 360-degree view over Seville's rooftops and the cathedral.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the panorama and sunset light; some note the walkway gets busy at the golden slot.
places.localTip: Time your ticket for sunset to shoot the cathedral silhouette, then stay for the evening light projection on the canopy.
places.story: From the top of the giant wooden Setas, Seville unrolls beneath you — a sea of terracotta roofs pierced by the Giralda. There's no higher accessible viewpoint in the old centre. As the sun sinks, the whole city turns rose-gold and the cathedral becomes a dark spire against the sky.

places.name: Puente de Triana (Isabel II)
places.address: Puente de Isabel II, 41010 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: Seville's oldest iron bridge, the perfect vantage for shooting the river, the Triana chapel and the old-city skyline.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the views both ways; some note traffic and crowds on the narrow walkways.
places.localTip: Shoot from the Triana side at dusk to catch the Capilla del Carmen and the river lights together.
places.story: Built in 1852 and modelled on a Parisian bridge that no longer exists, the Triana bridge is the oldest iron bridge in Spain. From its span you get the classic view of the river curving past the old city. At its Triana foot stands the bright little Capilla del Carmen, a favourite frame for photographers.

places.name: Patio de las Doncellas (Alcázar)
places.address: Patio de Banderas, s/n, 41004 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The Alcázar's central courtyard, with a long reflecting pool framed by intricate Mudéjar arches — a photographer's dream.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it jaw-dropping; the only issue is crowds, so the first morning slot is best for clean shots.
places.localTip: Book the first entry of the day and head straight here before tour groups arrive to catch the empty pool reflection.
places.story: The Court of the Maidens takes its dark name from a legend that the Christian kingdoms once paid the Moors a tribute of a hundred maidens. Its long central pool mirrors the lacework of arches above in perfect symmetry. Empty and still in the early morning, it's one of the most beautiful spaces in Spain.
places.highlight: Don't miss the reflecting pool before the crowds.

places.name: Calle Agua & Plaza de Doña Elvira
places.reviewSummary: A storybook street and tiled square in Santa Cruz, draped in bougainvillea with orange trees and a trickling fountain.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find it impossibly romantic and photogenic; some say it's tiny and busy midday.
places.localTip: Photograph it early morning when the light is soft and the bougainvillea glows against the whitewash, before tour groups arrive.
places.story: Calle Agua — Water Street — got its name from the old aqueduct pipes hidden in its walls that once fed the Alcázar's gardens. It opens onto Plaza de Doña Elvira, a tiled, orange-scented square said to be linked to the legend of Don Juan. Draped in bougainvillea, it's Santa Cruz at its most romantic.

places.name: Muelle de la Sal (riverbank)
places.address: Paseo Alcalde Marqués del Contadero, 41001 Sevilla, Spain
places.reviewSummary: The riverside walk by the Torre del Oro, ideal for golden-hour shots of the tower, the river and Triana glowing across the water.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the sunset views and calm; some note it's popular with joggers and couples at dusk.
places.localTip: Set up here about 30 minutes before sunset to catch the Torre del Oro and the Triana skyline reflected in the river.
places.story: This stretch of the Guadalquivir promenade, by the old Salt Wharf, gives you Seville's most cinematic golden hour. The Torre del Oro glows amber, boats drift past, and Triana lights up across the water. Locals come here to walk off the day's heat as the sky turns pink.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Torre del Oro reflection at sunset.
